utterances: Yes, that's right.
It takes four votes to grant an appeal.
And they would hear these arguments next fall?
That's correct.
And then get a decision by?
Well, you never know; two to three or four months.
Presumably, the first half of next year.
And tell us, at this point, there are an awful lot of states--a majority of them--that have parental notification laws, but they require a whole number of different things.
That's right.
There's always a lot of--this has been a disputed area.
It's about 33 states have laws on the books that require either notification or consent, one or the other.
Going back to the early '80s, the Supreme Courts have upheld these laws in principle.

However, they said in special situations, you always need to leave an option for a young girl to go to a judge and get a waiver, because you could imagine awful situations where, you know, there's been sexual abuse or any number of terrible things could have happened, and the young girl shouldn't be absolutely forced to notify her parents or get her parents' consent.
So the Supreme Court's always had this sort of compromise view, which is, yes, you can have these laws, but you also need to leave an option for the girl to get a waiver.
David Savage, thanks very much.
Thank you, Neal.
David Savage covers the Supreme Court for the Los Angeles Times.
He joined us form his office here in Washington, DC.
Well, to look at how these laws and these issues have played out in one state, joining us now is Maro Robbins, legal affairs reporter for the San Antonio Express-News.
He's with us from their office in Austin, Texas.
Nice to have you on the program.
Thank you.
What does the law in Texas on parental notification require?
The law here requires that abortion providers must give 48 hours' notice to the parents of a pregnant minor before actually performing the abortion.
And is there an exception for the health and the life of the mother?
That's right.

The law lets a minor go to court and ask a judge to let her get the procedure without telling her parents, but it requires the minor to prove to the judge that she is sufficiently well-informed about the decisions, is mature, or alternatively, that telling her parents might result in abuse.

The law itself was vague, and so it was up to the Texas Supreme Court to define it, and the majority of the court finally decided that a minor was well-informed if she had consulted a health-care provider about the status of the pregnancy and what was involved in an abortion, if she knew about the child support laws that required a father to help support a child, and she had considered alternatives to abortion, such as adoption or keeping the child, and then thirdly, that the minor had consulted a reliable source about possible emotional fallout from an abortion and whatever possible effect the abortion might have on the relationship with her parents and other family members.
Was this played out in a c--you know, on a sort of theoretical basis, challenging the law, or was there a case involved?

It's been implemented hundreds of times, so the first time that it went to court was in 2000 and was challenged, and that was the case of a 17-year-old high school senior who became pregnant, but had planned on going to college and having a career, and who also had religious parents who she believed might stop her from getting an abortion or might even disown her, and so she went to court and argued that she was well-informed, and to do that, she told the court that she had talked to a close relative who had had an abortion at 17.
She had talked to another teen-ager who had had an abortion.
She also talked to a married 15-year-old who was struggling to go to school and raise children.
Additionally, she had talked to the teacher of her high school parenting class and had met twice with a Planned Parenthood consultant--or counselor, sorry...
Yes.
...a Planned Parenthood counselor and had read their literature.
And additionally, she had seen the fetus on an ultrasound.
Ultimately, the court did decide that she was well-informed enough and did let her get the procedure, but it was not without some debate, and some judges felt that she wasn't well-informed.
Was this at a time when--I'm just curious--Priscilla Owen, a Texas Supreme Court justice--was she on the Supreme Court then?
Yes, she was.
In fact, she was among the judges who felt that the minor probably was not informed enough.

Priscilla Owen, I should point out, one of President Bush's disputed judicial nominees, and her appointment to, I think it's the 5th Circuit Court of Appeals in New Orleans, was confirmed earlier today by the US Senate.
That's sort of why I raised it.
How often do issues--do minors go to court to--you told us a hundred times it's been applied?

No, it's--at least the last numbers I saw were--actually are a few years old, but the law went into effect in '99, and by 2002, it had been used about 500 times, and there's not firm records on that, but that was the best guess that people put together by studying the issue.
And is this a live issue in the state of Texas?

Yes, there's still debate in the Legislature at this point, still bills that have been put as recently as this session that would take it a step further and would require both parents' actual permission.
Instead of just being notified, it would require parents to agree to the abortion.
Maro Robbins, thanks very much.
We appreciate your time today.
Thank you.
Maro Robbins covers the courts for the San Antonio Express-News, and he joined us from that paper's bureau in Austin, Texas.

When we come back from a short break, we'll talk with parenting experts about how notification laws play out between teens and their families, and really, we want to talk with you about what happens before the law is invoked, about communication with your children, and their communication with you.
If this happened to you, if you were in this situation, did you talk to your parents?
Did your daughter talk to you?
What did you talk about and what did you decide?
Give us a call.
Our number is (800) 989-8255.
That's (800) 989-TALK.
The e-mail address, totn@npr.org.
I'm Neal Conan.
We're back after the break.
It's TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
(Soundbite of music)
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
This fall, as we mentioned earlier, the Supreme Court will hear arguments in a case involving parental notification laws for girls seeking abortions.
The case is likely to arouse political passions on all sides, but that overlooks the far more personal terrain on which each of these decisions is made.
Every pregnant teen who considers an abortion lives within her own constellation of family values, relationships, pressures.
Today we want to hear those stories.

If you've ever had to go through this experience as a teen facing an unexpected pregnancy, as a parent who was told or wasn't, has a friend or family member who took part in this decision, give us a phone call; (800) 989-8255, (800) 989-TALK.
Our e-mail address is totn@npr.org.
And joining us now is Sarah Brown, director of the National Campaign to Prevent Teen Pregnancy.
She's talked with many teens and their parents around the delicate issues around an unplanned pregnancy, and she's here with us in Studio 3A.
Nice to see you again, Sarah.
Thanks.
What do you hear from teens about this dilemma?
Well, the overwhelming report from them is that when they realize they're pregnant and they think they have to reveal that to a parent, they're just terrified.
They're scared.
They're embarrassed.
They're enormously upset.
A young woman will commonly say, `I think my parents will kill me.
They may not mean that literally, but it's a measure of how absolutely frightened they are about this.
I mean, I've often thought that, you know, disappointing a parent is one of the primal fears of a child, even in the most disorganized family.
You know, to have your mother or father say, you know, `How could you do that?
I'm so disappointed,' for some teens will seem even more terrifying than the burden of maybe raising a child, because it's immediate.
It's, `I have to do this right now.
And it's simply overwhelming to them.
That's right.
Imagine having to say that you're pregnant, and, of course, sort of on the flip side, the parents are often unaware.
I mean, the majority of parents particularly of young teens aren't even aware that their children are having sex if they are.
So imagine if you're not even aware that your 15- or 16-year-old might be having sex, imagine how unaware you might be of them being pregnant.
So these early encounters when this issue is joined are some of the most difficult that any family faces, hands down.
What can parents do so that things--obviously, you help people--work to prevent teen-age pregnancies.
This is your job in life.
Right.
How do you work with parents to try to prevent this in the first place?
Well, I think the principal question a parent has to ask him or herself is: Do I want to be the one that my child can go to if this monster happens, if we have an unintended pregnancy at a young age?

And if the answer is yes, as much as it would upset me, as much as it might disappoint me, if I want to be the one or one of the first ones, what that means is I have to focus over many years in talking openly and candidly and warmly with my children sex, love and relationships.
Now that doesn't mean I don't have a point of view.
It doesn't mean I don't have values to communicate and so forth.
But we have quite a bit of data showing that close parent-child relationships over many years, about lots of things, not just sex, that seems to be one of the most protective things parents can do.
So, you know, it's the same old message.
Parents have to be parents.

They have to know where their young people are, know who their friends are, talk openly and candidly about these issues that face their children, so that when the unexpected happens--and it does--you know, we have about 850,000 pregnancies among teen-agers each year in this country--that that parent is not considered a source of abject terror and fear; maybe embarrassment, but not terror.
We really want to hear from listeners this hour, and let's begin with Terry.
Terry's calling us from San Antonio, Texas.
Good afternoon.
Afternoon.

What I wanted to share was that some decades ago now, for lack of wanting to reveal my age, I was in a similar situation; doing well in school, first boyfriend, accident; absolutely never occurred to me to discuss this situation with my parents.
I knew where they stood on the subject.
I knew what my decision was going to be, and life has been peaceful in my house, and we get along, have a close relationship with my parents to this day.
I feel that I preserved that relationship in part because I did not share this information with them.
Ever?
